text: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) have been associated with numerous adverse health effects and are now of widespread concern. Identifying sources of PFAS contamination at ultra-trace regulatory limits (ng/L) traditionally requires costly off-site separation and analysis through methods such as chromatography and mass spectrometry. Our work here shows that PFAS in aqueous solution can be detected at high sensitivity in situ with amplifying fluorescent polymers (AFPs) prepared from highly fluorinated poly(p-phenylene ethynylene) (PPE) and polyfluorene (PF) derivatives containing specific selectors such as pyridines. Binding between the Py containing AFPs and the acidic PFAS like PFOA and PFOS results in proton transfer in addition to the analyte being sequestered into the FP backbone, the resulting pyridinium contributing to a large red shift in emission while being sequestered within the fluorous regions, and further enhancing the shift through the conjugated backbone as a trap based emission process analogous to a TICT emission mechanism. Thin film and aqueous solution based conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPdots) embodiments of this sensor have a detection limit of approximately 1 ppb and 100 ppt, respectively, while lacking selectivity to non-fluorinated non-acidic species and displaying robust performance through various solution background testing including milliQ, DI, and well water streams. Our sensor technology presents an on site aqueous solution based sensor that is scalable to analyze and protect distributed water resources.
explanation: Highly technical, domain-specific language with plausible experimental details and a coherent scientific narrative; this tone is typical of human-authored abstracts, though advanced AI could mimic it.
